---
title: NY HP Rate Design Analysis
toc: true
reference-location: margin
fig-cap-location: bottom
appendix-style: default
citation-location: document
citation:
  container-title: Switchbox
format:
  html:
    page-layout: full
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

This notebook analyzes CAIRO simulation outputs for New York heat pump rate design. It produces two families of
visualization:

1. **Bill change histograms** — weighted distributions of annual total energy bill changes after switching to heat
   pumps, by population subgroup.
2. **Median electric bill component breakdown** — stacked bar chart decomposing the weighted-median electric bill into
   supply, delivery volumetric, and delivery fixed charges for current HVAC vs heat pump scenarios.

## Setup

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

## Parameters

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

## Constants

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

## Data ingest

The master bills tables are pre-built, Hive-partitioned parquet datasets that contain per-building monthly and annual
bills with fully decomposed electric components (fixed, delivery volumetric, supply), gas, oil, and propane — plus
ResStock metadata columns — across all NY utilities.

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

## Median energy bill components

Stacked bar chart of energy bill components (electric delivery fixed / delivery volumetric / supply + gas) for the
weighted-median natgas-heated customer under current HVAC, and that same building after switching to a heat pump. Both
bars show the same building so the reader can see gas costs shrink and electric costs grow for one household.

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### Utility-level

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

1. Keep only natgas-heated buildings with zero propane and zero oil bills (\~3.3% of ConEd natgas buildings are
   dropped). This ensures the visible bar components (electric + gas) equal the full energy total with nothing hidden.
2. Pre-filter the HP scenario to the same natgas-only building IDs so we can look up individual buildings.
3. Reuse the same building (not a new median) for the HP bar. Two different buildings at the median would have different
   gas baselines, making it impossible to see gas costs shrink after electrification. Showing the same household keeps
   the comparison apples-to-apples.

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### Statewide

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

1. Same natgas-only filter as the ConEd cell. Statewide, \~2.7% of natgas buildings are dropped.
2. Pre-filter the HP scenario to the same natgas-only building IDs so we can look up individual buildings.
3. Same-building lookup (see ConEd annotation above).

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

## Bill change distributions

Weighted histograms of annual total energy bill change (before vs after switching to heat pumps) under default rates.
The master bills `energy_total_bill` already includes electric, gas, oil, and propane costs.

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### ConEd

#### Non-HP households

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

#### Natural gas heated

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

#### Oil / propane heated

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

#### Electric resistance heated

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

#### Quadrant summary bars

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### Statewide

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

## Bill Alignment Test (BAT) results

The master BAT table contains per-building annual bill alignment values decomposed into delivery, supply, and total
components. A positive `BAT_percustomer` value means the customer's bill exceeds their marginal cost of service — they
cross-subsidize other customers. A negative value means they pay less than their cost — others cross-subsidize them.

We look at all customers under current rates (upgrade 0 / run 1+2) to quantify how current rate structures misalign
bills with cost of service. `BAT_percustomer_total` is the sum of delivery and supply components. The unified histograms
show the full population; the faceted versions break the distribution down by heating type (fossil fuel, electric
resistance, heat pump).

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### ConEd

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### Statewide

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### Summary statistics

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### Impact of tech-specific rates on fossil fuel cross-subsidization

What happens to fossil fuel customers' cross-subsidization if tech-specific rates zero out the net overpayment of HP
customers, ER customers, or both? We spread the released overpayment uniformly across fossil fuel households and report
the shift in their net, median annual BAT, and median monthly BAT (over a 6-month heating season).

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

## Revenue requirement composition

Each utility's total residential revenue requirement — the amount it must recover from residential ratepayers — breaks
into three pieces:

1. **Delivery (Rate Case)** — the base T&D revenue requirement set in the utility's most recent rate case.
2. **Delivery Top-Ups** — additional delivery-side charges for policy programs (SBC, CES delivery, EV make-ready, DLM),
   DER credit recovery (VDER), and sunk-cost recovery (COVID arrears, late-payment waivers).
3. **Supply** — energy commodity, merchant function charge, and CES supply surcharge.

Data comes from the computed revenue requirement YAMLs in `rate_design/hp_rates/ny/config/rev_requirement/`.

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

### Revenue requirement vs. marginal cost allocation

The BAT decomposes each customer's cost of service into **marginal cost** (economic burden: Σ load × MC) and **residual
cost** (revenue requirement minus total marginal costs, allocated per customer). Because we run paired CAIRO simulations
— delivery-only (run 1) and delivery+supply (run 2) — we can decompose both marginal cost and residual cost into
delivery and supply components. This lets us compare the revenue requirement breakdown against the BAT's cost allocation
at a finer grain.

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```

```{python}
27 files left unchanged
```
